videos.Title: Claude Code Loops in 7 Minutes
videos.Description:
Claude Code “Loop” Scheduling: Recurring AI Tasks in Your Session

The script explains Claude Code’s new “Loop” feature (an evolution of the Ralph Wiggins technique) for running recurring prompts that help verify work and prevent premature task completion. Loop can schedule tasks for up to three days using natural language or a /loop command, with a minimum one-minute interval, and it shows the scheduled prompt, notes, and auto-expiry. Examples include looping over PRs to fix build issues, summarizing tagged Slack posts via MCP, creating daily Git history recaps saved to the desktop, and scraping Hacker News with Firecrawl then emailing summaries via a connected Gmail CLI skill. Loops are scoped to the active Claude Code session and stop when the session closes, include up to a 10% scheduling offset to avoid synchronized API spikes, and can be disabled via a flag; for durable scheduling, the script suggests the desktop app feature or GitHub Actions.


FOLLOW ME
→ Website: https://dub.sh/dev-digest
→ X/Twitter: https://dub.sh/dd-x
→ GitHub: https://git.new/devdigest

→ Screen Studio (screen recording): https://dub.sh/dd-screenstudio

→ Railway (deployment): https://dub.sh/dd-railway

videos.Title: 12 Months of Claude Code Lessons in 46 Minutes
videos.Description:
52 Claude Code lessons from 12 months of building (beginner, intermediate and advanced). Covering Claude.md setup, custom slash commands, MCPs, Agent skills, workflows for building, testing, planning, multi-agent development, git worktrees, Ralph Wiggum loops, hooks, and more.

Want to go even deeper on Claude Code with hands-on help? 

Check out Claude Code for Beginners (2 Day Intensive): https://maven.com/alpha-clarity/claude-code-for-beginners?promoCode=YOUTUBE (20% off with code YOUTUBE)

Join my 8 day AI Coding Accelerator: https://maven.com/alpha-clarity/ship-with-claude-code-replit?promoCode=YOUTUBE (20% off with code YOUTUBE)

Scholarship Application Form: https://forms.gle/tAhxqD8TTFNrfJAj6
--
12 months ago, I was just prompting and praying with Claude Code, barely scratching the surface of what it could do.

Fast forward to today, and I can run multiple Claude Codes to work on a week's worth of features at once, build custom commands and skills tailored to my exact workflows, and delegate my to-do list to Claude and come back to PRs ready to review.

In this video, I'm sharing 52 specific lessons about how to get the most out of Claude Code, organized into three levels: Beginner, Intermediate, and Master.

🎯 What You'll Learn:
✅ Level 1 — Beginner (Foundations):
Installation options: terminal, desktop app, IDE, and mobile 
Best models and modes to use (and why I default to Opus) 
The Claude.md file and 7 things to put in it 
Context window management and avoiding auto-compaction 
Screenshot debugging, Git management, and checkpoints

✅ Level 2 — Intermediate (Customizations & Workflows): 
Custom slash commands, MCPs, skills, plugins, and subagents 
The RPIT loop (Research, Plan, Implement, Test) for every feature 
Spec-driven development and issue-based development with GitHub 
Verifying Claude's work at higher levels of abstraction 
Auto-documenting your project and iteratively improving your Claude.md

✅ Level 3 — Master (Advanced Workflows): 
Multi-Clauding with git worktrees 
Ralph Wiggum loops for autonomous iteration 
Feedback loops to help Claude self-improve over time 
Async development via the web app, GitHub Actions, and Slack Agent teams, 
Pre-configured permissions, and hooks

videos.Title: Finding the Edges of Claude Code
videos.Description: What happens when you push Claude Code to its limits? Co-founder of Rōnin Consulting, Byron McClain, found out firsthand when he used the Ralph Wiggum Loop to port Command & Conquer: Red Alert from Windows to Mac... a language he'd never built in, on a game he'd never developed. The result? A working game, a lot of crashed builds, and a clear picture of where AI-assisted development actually stands today. In this episode, Byron breaks down exactly how he structured his tasks, what he got wrong the first time, and why experienced developers aren't going anywhere; they're just going to be managing agents instead of writing every line.

videos.Title: My 5 steps to build a Coding-Pipeline for Claude-Code
videos.Description:
How to build an AI pipeline that generates production-quality code.

In this video I show the structured approach I use to build an agentic development pipeline that generates the same code I would normally write manually.

The key idea: combine Event Modeling, a clear architectural blueprint, and an AI agent loop that implements your system slice by slice.

This approach allows teams to generate large parts of a system automatically, while still keeping full architectural control.

After a few iterations, pipelines like this can significantly speed up development and even run parts of the system creation on auto-pilot.

The 5 Steps

1. Start with Event Modeling
Event Modeling gives you a clear system overview: commands, events, views, and flows.
Without this structure, AI tends to generate inconsistent code.

2. Define a Blueprint
Use your existing codebase as a reference architecture, or generate a blueprint from a trusted source such as the AxonIQ Platform.

3. Generate Transformations
Use AI to create the transformation logic that turns the blueprint into code.
In this example I generate the required Claude Code skills.

4. Run the Agent over the Event Model
The agent runs in a loop over the slices defined in the Event Model, implementing each piece of behavior step by step.

5. Refine
Improve the blueprint, the prompts, and the transformations.
The pipeline quickly becomes more reliable with each iteration.

In the video I also demonstrate the Ralph Loop technique, which allows slices to be generated automatically — a very elegant way of structuring the workflow.

Learn Event Modeling

If you want to learn Event Modeling and build pipelines like this with your team:

I run Event Modeling workshops where we:

• model real systems
• define the architectural blueprint
• build the agentic pipeline step by step

Event Modeling Book:
https://www.eventsourcingbook.com

The Online Course to learn the process step by step:
https://www.eventsourcingcourse.com

videos.Title: Zero Touch Marketing Is Real Now
videos.Description:
Someone added an event to Airtable. Within minutes, a full marketing campaign was live — ad copy in 5 platform tones including Taglish, AI-generated event posters, a published Wix event page, social-ready content, and a branded dashboard to monitor it all. No human touched it.
In Video 1, I was the orchestrator. In Video 2, I automated the orchestrator. In Video 3, there IS no orchestrator. The pipeline triggers itself, validates itself, and publishes to 5 platforms.
In this video, I show you:
→ Airtable webhook triggers that fire the entire pipeline automatically (Z-thread — zero-touch automation)
→ How to give AI "eyes" with dev-browser — it screenshots its own UI and fixes layout issues
→ The design-reference skill — my AI screenshots my live website and matches the brand
→ Visual QA at 3 responsive breakpoints (mobile, tablet, desktop)
→ YOLO mode with safety nets — full autonomy with git checkpoints and hooks
→ F-thread (fusion) — multiple agents design the dashboard, I combine the best of each
→ A real live demo: adding an event to Airtable and watching the campaign generate end-to-end
This is Part 3 of a 3-part series:
📌 Video 1: Build the architecture with sub-agents
📌 Video 2: Automate the build with a for-loop (Ralph Wiggum technique)
📌 Video 3 (this one): Zero-touch marketing — Airtable triggers the entire pipeline
2 new thread patterns demonstrated:

Z-thread — zero-touch: Airtable webhook triggers pipeline, pipeline validates and publishes with no human review
F-thread — fusion: multiple agents produce dashboard designs, best elements combined into one

Ralph upgrades in this video:

Browser feedback loop: Screenshot → Evaluate → Fix → Re-screenshot (for frontend features)
YOLO permissions with git checkpoints (full autonomy + safety nets)
Regression testing: after each new feature, re-test 3 random completed features

videos.Title: The Secret Mechanics: How Do These New Killer Drones ACTUALLY Work?
videos.Description:
📝 Get 3x more interviews with https://resumeflow.app
🥅 Accomplish your goals with our coaching portal at https://thrive.techcoachralph.dev
🏖 Exclusive Vibe Code & Chill at https://techcoachralph.dev 

A short, clear breakdown of the first reported combat use of the US "Lucas" one-way attack drone and why it matters. This video unpacks how a reverse-engineered Shahed-style drone, built by a small firm, changed the cost dynamics of modern strike warfare—and why that should make everyone uneasy.

What you'll learn:
- The basics: what Lucas stands for (Low-Cost Unmanned Combat Attack System), who built it (Spectreworks/Phoenix), and how it compares to Iran's Shahed-136.
- Capabilities: reported range (500–1,000 miles), ~40 lb payload, truck- or ship-launched catapult systems, autonomous GPS navigation, loitering and swarm tactics.
- Cost realities: US government estimates (~$35k/unit) vs public speculation ($10k–$15k civilian range) and why that price drop matters.
- Targeting and connectivity: possibilities for human-in-the-loop control using Starlink-like links, and worrying mentions of AI-assisted targeting ("Claude for government").

Strategic and ethical implications:
- Proliferation risk: low-cost designs lower the barrier for copycats and non-state actors.
- Industry shift: rapid fielding by small companies undercuts large defense primes and speeds deployment.
- Legal and moral questions: autonomous strikes, oversight gaps, and who is accountable when AI nudges targeting decisions.

Why this is urgent: War becomes cheaper to prosecute, which changes incentives for escalation and increases the chance of wider adoption. This isn't just a weapons story—it's about policy, industry, and the future of accountability in conflict.

If you want concise analysis, clear facts, and the key quotes that expose the unknowns around Lucas and the broader debate on autonomous strikes, this episode lays it out.

Hashtags:
LucasDrone KamikazeDrone Shahed136 Spectreworks DroneWarfare AutonomousWeapons AIinWarfare SwarmTactics OperationEpicFury DroneProliferation ClaudeAI Starlink LowCostWeapons UnmannedCombatAttack

#Lucasdrone #kamikazedrone #Shahed136 #Spectreworks #onewaydrone #lowcostunmannedcombatattacksystem #OperationEpicFury #dronewarfare2026 #poormanscruisemissile #autonomousdrones #droneswarmtactics #droneproliferation
